summary: The spinal cord extends from the foramen magnum to L1-L2 in adults, and its terminal end is the conus medullaris.
The spinal cord extends to L4-L5 in adults.
The spinal cord extends to S2 in adults and terminates as the filum terminale.
The cervical enlargement (C4-T1) supplies the upper limb and the lumbosacral enlargement (L2-S3) supplies the lower limb.
The thoracic region supplies the upper limb and the cervical region supplies the lower limb.
Both enlargements supply the trunk musculature.
In Brown-Sequard syndrome, there is ipsilateral motor loss and proprioception loss with contralateral pain and temperature loss.
In Brown-Sequard syndrome, all sensory and motor losses are contralateral.
Brown-Sequard syndrome causes bilateral loss of all modalities below the lesion.
The lateral horn is present at T1-L2 levels and contains preganglionic sympathetic neurons.
The lateral horn is present at all spinal cord levels.
The lateral horn contains postganglionic parasympathetic neurons.
The dorsal columns carry fine touch, vibration, and proprioception, while the spinothalamic tract carries pain and temperature.
The dorsal columns carry pain and temperature, while the spinothalamic tract carries proprioception.
Both the dorsal columns and spinothalamic tract carry the same modalities.
Lumbar puncture is performed at L3-L4 or L4-L5 interspace because the spinal cord ends at L1-L2.
Lumbar puncture is performed at T12-L1 interspace.
Lumbar puncture can be safely performed at any spinal level.
The cauda equina consists of lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal nerve roots descending below the conus medullaris.
The cauda equina is a continuation of the spinal cord below L2.
The cauda equina contains only motor nerve roots.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Revise the clinical correlations.
Excellent understanding of spinal cord anatomy!
